title: The attack on Epiphanius as a plagiarist of Origen is an outrage on the Bishops generally. Origen never wrote 6000 books.

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Who are these men who are wont to dispute at such great length in the churches, and to write books, and whose discourses and writings are taken wholly from Origen; these men who are afraid of their literary thefts becoming known, and shew ingratitude towards their master, and who therefore deter men of simple mind from reading him?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You ought to mention them by name, and designate the men themselves.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are the reverend bishops Anastasius and Theophilus, Venerius and Chromatius, and the whole council of the Catholics both in the East and in the West, who publicly denounce him as a heretic, to be esteemed to be plagiarists of his books?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are we to believe that, when they preach in the churches, they do not preach the mysteries of the Scriptures, but merely repeat what they have stolen from Origen?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it not enough for you to disparage them all in general, but you must specially aim the spear of your pen against a reverend and eminent Bishop of the church?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Who is this who considers that he has a necessity laid on him of reviling Origen, as the Gospel which he must preach among all nations and tongues? this man who proclaimed in the audience of a vast multitude of the brethren that he had read six thousand of his books?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You yourself were in the very centre of that multitude and company of the brethren, when, as he complains in his letter, the monstrous doctrines of Origen were enlarged upon by you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it to be imputed to him as a crime that he knows the Greek, the Syrian, the Hebrew, the Egyptian, and in part also the Latin language?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then, I suppose, the Apostles and Apostolic men, who spoke with tongues, are to be condemned; and you who know two languages may deride me who know three.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But as for the six thousand books which you pretend that he has read, who will believe that you are speaking the truth, or that he was capable of telling such a lie?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If indeed Origen had written six thousand books, it is possible that a man of great learning, who had been trained from his infancy in sacred literature might have read books alien from his own convictions, because he had an inquiring spirit and a love of learning.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But how could be read what Origen never wrote?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Count up the index contained in the third volume of Eusebius, in which is his life of Pamphilus: you will not find, I do not say six thousand, but not a third of that number of books.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have by me the letter of the above named Pontiff, in which he gives his answer to this calumny of yours uttered when you were still in the East; and it confutes this most manifest falsehood with the open countenance of truth.
